name: Gadolinium
name_origin: Named after the mineral gadolinite, which itself was named for Finnish chemist Johan Gadolin.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moderately abundant lanthanide (~6.2 ppm in crust); always found combined with other lanthanides in monazite, bastnaesite, gadolinite, and xenotime ores.

compounds.name: Gadolinium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: White solid with high neutron absorption cross-section; used in nuclear reactor shielding and as a precursor for MRI contrast agents.

compounds.name: Gadolinium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: White hygroscopic salt; precursor for gadolinium-based MRI contrast agents and high-purity metal.

compounds.name: Gadopentetic acid (Magnevist)
compounds.summary: Octacoordinate Gd(III) chelate used as a paramagnetic intravenous MRI contrast agent.

compounds.name: Gadolinium gallium garnet (GGG)
compounds.summary: Single-crystal substrate for magnetic-bubble memory devices and a host for solid-state lasers.

uses: Paramagnetic contrast agents for medical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
Neutron-shielding and burnable poisons in nuclear reactor control rods
Magnetocaloric refrigeration near room temperature (Gd has a Curie point of 293 K)
Phosphor host in colour TV tubes and X-ray intensifying screens (Gd2O2S)
Microwave electronics components (gadolinium gallium garnet substrates)
Alloying additive that improves workability of iron, chromium, and related metals
reactivity_summary: Silvery-white ductile metal that is relatively stable in dry air but tarnishes rapidly in moist air, forming a flaky oxide that promotes further corrosion. Reacts slowly with water and dissolves in dilute acids. Ferromagnetic just below room temperature.
isotopes_summary: Gadolinium has six stable isotopes (Gd-154, 155, 156, 157, 158, 160) and one primordial radioisotope Gd-152 (alpha emitter, half-life 1.08x10^14 years). Gd-157 has the highest known thermal neutron capture cross-section of any stable nuclide.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Gadolinium
